section: 1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras
title: Block construction for asymptotic density counterexamples
summary: Use rapidly growing blocks so the latest block dominates all previous blocks; alternate behavior by blocks to destroy the density of intersections.
proof_idea: Prove structural set properties directly from closure rules; use generators/countable bases; build counterexamples by violating finite or countable closure.
exam_use: Exercise 1.1.5 and density/limit counterexamples.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records

title: Borel sigma-field from half-open rectangles
summary: Show half-open rectangles are Borel, then express rational open rectangles as countable unions of half-open rectangles; use the countable rational base.
proof_idea: Prove structural set properties directly from closure rules; use generators/countable bases; build counterexamples by violating finite or countable closure.
exam_use: Exercises 1.1.2 and 1.1.3; product-measure generation arguments.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras
title: Countable/co-countable probability measure
summary: To verify the countable/co-countable construction, split disjoint families into the cases where all sets are countable or exactly one set is co-countable.
proof_idea: Prove structural set properties directly from closure rules; use generators/countable bases; build counterexamples by violating finite or countable closure.
exam_use: Exercise 1.1.1 and any probability-space verification on unusual sigma-fields.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras
title: Increasing union of sigma-fields can fail countable closure
summary: Use finite initial-coordinate sigma-fields on N; their union contains every finite set but misses an infinite non-cofinite set such as the evens.
proof_idea: Prove structural set properties directly from closure rules; use generators/countable bases; build counterexamples by violating finite or countable closure.
exam_use: Exercise 1.1.4; distinguishing algebras from sigma-fields.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Discrete probability spaces
summary: On a countable sample space, assign nonnegative masses p(omega) summing to 1, and define P(A) as the sum over omega in A.
proof_idea: Countable additivity reduces to rearranging nonnegative series.
exam_use: Useful for checking intuition before proving the analogous statement for general measures.
pitfalls: If the sample space is uncountable, point masses need not determine the whole law.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Continuity of measure
summary: For increasing events A_n, P(union A_n) is the limit of P(A_n). For decreasing events A_n with finite first measure, P(intersection A_n) is the limit of P(A_n).
proof_idea: Write increasing differences as disjoint pieces; for decreasing sets apply the increasing result to complements or differences.
exam_use: Essential for limits of events, almost sure convergence criteria, and first Borel-Cantelli arguments.
pitfalls: For decreasing continuity, the finite-measure condition matters for general measures; it is automatic under probability measures.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Probability space
summary: A probability model is a triple (Omega, F, P): outcomes, a sigma-field of events, and a countably additive probability measure with total mass 1.
proof_idea: Most elementary probability identities are consequences of complement closure, countable union closure, and countable additivity.
exam_use: Use this as the opening language for proofs involving events, limsup/liminf events, and Borel-Cantelli later in Chapter 2.
pitfalls: Do not treat arbitrary subsets as events unless F is the full power set; measurability is part of the model.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Multidimensional distribution functions
summary: A function on R^d satisfying monotonicity over rectangles, right-continuity, and correct limits defines a probability measure on Borel R^d.
proof_idea: Use rectangle increments and an extension theorem from a semialgebra of rectangles.
exam_use: This underlies joint distributions and later independence statements.
pitfalls: Coordinatewise monotonicity alone is not enough; rectangle probabilities must be nonnegative.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Extension from semialgebras
summary: A measure-like set function on a semialgebra can be extended when it has the right countable additivity/continuity behavior on the generating class.
proof_idea: Pass from semialgebra pieces to finite disjoint unions, then use approximation and countable additivity to extend.
exam_use: Explains why specifying probabilities on intervals or rectangles is enough in many probability models.
pitfalls: Do not assume every finitely additive premeasure extends; the countable condition is the serious part.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Sigma-field closure rules
summary: A sigma-field is closed under complements and countable unions, hence also under countable intersections and finite set operations.
proof_idea: Use De Morgan's laws to derive intersection closure from complement and union closure.
exam_use: When proving a random object is measurable, show that a convenient generating class of inverse images lies in F.
pitfalls: A countable union is allowed; an arbitrary uncountable union need not be in the sigma-field.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Stieltjes measures from distribution-like functions
summary: A nondecreasing right-continuous function with suitable limits defines a unique measure on the Borel line via interval increments.
proof_idea: First define the measure on intervals, verify finite additivity and continuity, then extend to the generated sigma-field.
exam_use: This is the bridge from CDFs to probability measures on R.
pitfalls: Right-continuity is not cosmetic; it pins down the measure on half-infinite intervals and atoms.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Atoms are jumps of the CDF
summary: For a real random variable, P(X = x) equals the jump F(x) - F(x-) of its distribution function.
proof_idea: Compare events {X <= x} and {X < x}; use monotone convergence from below for F(x-).
exam_use: Useful for mixed discrete-continuous examples and for proving a CDF has at most countably many discontinuities.
pitfalls: A continuous CDF does not imply a density exists; the Cantor distribution is the standard warning.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Every valid CDF determines a law
summary: Any function with the standard CDF properties is the distribution function of a unique probability measure on R.
proof_idea: Construct a Stieltjes measure and check that its half-line values match the function.
exam_use: Lets you prove convergence claims by identifying limiting CDFs, provided the limit is a valid CDF at continuity points.
pitfalls: A pointwise limit of CDFs may fail to be a CDF if total mass escapes to infinity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Distribution function properties
summary: A CDF is nondecreasing, right-continuous, tends to 0 at -infinity, and tends to 1 at +infinity.
proof_idea: Monotonicity follows from event inclusion; right-continuity follows from continuity of probability for decreasing events.
exam_use: Use these properties to test whether a proposed function can be a CDF.
pitfalls: Left-continuity is generally false; jumps represent atoms.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Standard normal tail estimate
summary: The standard normal upper tail has exponential decay comparable to phi(x)/x for positive x.
proof_idea: Integrate the normal density and compare using monotonicity or one integration-by-parts style bound.
exam_use: Appears in Brownian motion, LIL-style bounds, and CLT error estimates.
pitfalls: Keep track of whether a bound is one-sided or two-sided; prelim solutions often lose constants harmlessly but not exponents.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Probability integral transform
summary: If X has a continuous CDF F, then F(X) is uniform on (0,1) under suitable strictness/continuity conditions.
proof_idea: Compute P(F(X) <= u) by translating through quantiles; handle flat parts carefully.
exam_use: Useful for constructing random variables from uniform variables and for distribution transformations.
pitfalls: Continuity alone needs care when F has flat intervals; use generalized inverses for the clean general statement.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations
title: Countability of CDF jumps
summary: Group jumps by size bigger than 1/m inside bounded intervals; each group is finite because total CDF increase is at most one.
proof_idea: Convert distribution questions into CDF/event identities; use jumps for discontinuities; use inverse transformations and normal-tail bounds for explicit calculations.
exam_use: Exercise 1.2.3; monotone-function discontinuity arguments.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

summary: For Y=g(X) with g increasing, compute F_Y(y)=P(X <= g^{-1}(y)) and differentiate to get f_X(g^{-1}(y))/g'(g^{-1}(y)).

pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

title: Piecewise random variable measurability
summary: For Z = X on A and Y on A^c, write {Z <= x} as (A cap {X <= x}) union (A^c cap {Y <= x}).
proof_idea: Convert distribution questions into CDF/event identities; use jumps for discontinuities; use inverse transformations and normal-tail bounds for explicit calculations.
exam_use: Exercise 1.2.1; patching random variables on measurable events.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

summary: For Y=X^2, use F_Y(y)=P(-sqrt(y)<=X<=sqrt(y)) and differentiate to get [f(sqrt(y))+f(-sqrt(y))]/(2sqrt(y)).
proof_idea: Convert distribution questions into CDF/event identities; use jumps for discontinuities; use inverse transformations and normal-tail bounds for explicit calculations.

pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

section: 1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization
title: Lower semicontinuity via closed sublevel sets
summary: A function is lower semicontinuous iff all sets {f <= a} are closed; sequential closure proves one direction and contradiction via subsequences proves the other.
proof_idea: Check inverse images on generating classes; express measurability through countable rational cuts; prove closure under limits and construct Borel representatives.
exam_use: Exercise 1.3.5; proving semicontinuous functions are measurable.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization
title: Constructive representation of sigma(X)-measurable functions
summary: Quantize Y into dyadic bins, represent each bin as {X in B}, build Borel simple functions f_n, then take a measurable pointwise limit f with Y=f(X).
proof_idea: Check inverse images on generating classes; express measurability through countable rational cuts; prove closure under limits and construct Borel representatives.
exam_use: Exercises 1.3.8 and 1.3.9; conditional-expectation foundations.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization
title: Discontinuity set from upper and lower local envelopes
summary: Define local sup and inf over balls, take limits as radius decreases, and identify discontinuities as the set where the limiting envelopes differ.
proof_idea: Check inverse images on generating classes; express measurability through countable rational cuts; prove closure under limits and construct Borel representatives.
exam_use: Exercise 1.3.6; measurability of discontinuity sets.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization
title: Rational cut proof for sum measurability
summary: Write {X+Y<x} as the countable union over q in Q of {X<q} cap {Y<x-q}.
proof_idea: Check inverse images on generating classes; express measurability through countable rational cuts; prove closure under limits and construct Borel representatives.
exam_use: Exercise 1.3.2; direct measurability of sums.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Composition of measurable maps
summary: If X is measurable and f is measurable, then f(X) is measurable.
proof_idea: Inverse images compose: {f(X) in A} equals {X in f^{-1}(A)}.
exam_use: Use this to justify transformations such as X^2, exp(X), indicators, vectors, sums, limsup, and liminf.
pitfalls: Continuity is sufficient for Borel measurability, but not necessary.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Limits preserve measurability
summary: Supremum, infimum, limsup, liminf, and pointwise limits of measurable real functions are measurable.
proof_idea: Express events such as {sup X_n <= a} or {limsup X_n < a} using countable unions/intersections of measurable events.
exam_use: Crucial for defining almost sure convergence and stopping-time events.
pitfalls: The countability of the sequence matters; arbitrary suprema over uncountable families can fail measurability.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Checking measurability on generators
summary: To prove X is measurable into a generated sigma-field, it is enough to check inverse images of a generating class.
proof_idea: The inverse-image operation preserves complements and countable unions, so the checked class expands to a sigma-field.
exam_use: Use half-lines for real variables and rectangles for vector-valued variables.
pitfalls: The generator must actually generate the target sigma-field; checking too small a class proves too little.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Arithmetic of random variables
summary: Finite sums, products, maxima, minima, and continuous functions of random variables are random variables.
proof_idea: View (X,Y) as a measurable vector and compose with continuous maps such as addition or multiplication.
exam_use: Often needed before taking expectations of constructed quantities.
pitfalls: Do not use arithmetic closure for extended real expressions without checking undefined forms like infinity minus infinity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Random variables as measurable maps
summary: A random variable is a measurable map from the sample space to a measurable state space.
proof_idea: Check inverse images of measurable target sets.
exam_use: This is the language needed for sigma(X), conditional expectation, and stochastic processes.
pitfalls: Do not confuse a random variable with its distribution; different variables may have the same law.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Functions measurable with respect to sigma(X)
summary: A random variable Y is sigma(X)-measurable exactly when it can be represented as a measurable function of X.
proof_idea: Approximate Y by simple functions and show each sigma(X)-measurable indicator factors through X.
exam_use: This is a foundation for conditional expectation as a function of observed information.
pitfalls: The representing function is only determined up to the distribution of X, not necessarily pointwise everywhere.

section: 1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation
title: Dyadic lower simple approximations
summary: Approximate f>=0 by 2^{-n} floor(2^n f); the approximants increase to f and their integrals give monotone sums over dyadic level sets.
proof_idea: Approximate integrable functions by simple, step, and continuous functions; use monotone simple approximations and reduction to step functions for oscillatory integrals.
exam_use: Exercise 1.4.2 and simple-function approximation.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

summary: Approximate an integrable function by simple functions, approximate measurable sets by finite interval unions, then round step-function corners.
proof_idea: Approximate integrable functions by simple, step, and continuous functions; use monotone simple approximations and reduction to step functions for oscillatory integrals.
exam_use: Exercise 1.4.3; density of nice functions in L1 on R.

title: Riemann-Lebesgue by step-function reduction

proof_idea: Approximate integrable functions by simple, step, and continuous functions; use monotone simple approximations and reduction to step functions for oscillatory integrals.
exam_use: Exercise 1.4.4; oscillatory integral estimates.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation
title: Zero integral of nonnegative function implies zero a.e.
summary: Use sets {f > 1/n}; each has zero measure because integral dominates (1/n) times its measure, and their union is {f>0}.
proof_idea: Approximate integrable functions by simple, step, and continuous functions; use monotone simple approximations and reduction to step functions for oscillatory integrals.
exam_use: Exercise 1.4.1; null-set proofs.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Integrable signed functions
summary: A signed measurable function is integrable when the integrals of its positive and negative parts are finite, equivalently the integral of its absolute value is finite.
proof_idea: Define integral as integral of positive part minus integral of negative part.
exam_use: Before using linearity or dominated convergence, check integrability conditions.
pitfalls: Finite positive and negative parts are needed; infinity minus infinity is not an acceptable value.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Linearity and order properties of the integral
summary: For integrable functions, the integral is linear, monotone, and respects almost-everywhere equality.
proof_idea: Prove first for simple functions, extend to nonnegative functions, then signed integrable functions.
exam_use: Use to split expectations, compare nonnegative variables, and justify replacing variables by a.s. equal versions.
pitfalls: Linearity can fail as a finite statement if one side involves undefined infinities.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Integral of a nonnegative function
summary: The integral of a nonnegative measurable function is the supremum of integrals of simple functions bounded above by it.
proof_idea: Approximate from below by simple functions and use monotone behavior.
exam_use: This definition makes monotone convergence natural rather than surprising.
pitfalls: The value may be infinite; do not subtract two infinite nonnegative integrals.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Simple functions
summary: A simple function takes finitely many values and is the starting point for defining the Lebesgue integral.
proof_idea: Define the integral as the sum of value times measure over level sets, then extend by monotone approximation.
exam_use: When stuck on an integral proof, first prove it for indicators, then simple functions, then limits.
pitfalls: Representations of a simple function are not unique; use canonical level sets to avoid ambiguity.

summary: If sum integral |f_n| is finite, then H=sum |f_n| is integrable; dominated convergence applies to partial sums.
proof_idea: Use Holder/Minkowski, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and truncation to prove norm, series, and continuity properties.

summary: Upper bound by essential supremum; lower bound using sets where |f| exceeds M-epsilon and their positive probability.
proof_idea: Use Holder/Minkowski, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and truncation to prove norm, series, and continuity properties.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Bounded convergence theorem
summary: On a finite-measure space, uniformly bounded functions converging pointwise have integrals converging to the integral of the limit.
proof_idea: Reduce to dominated convergence with a constant dominating function, or prove using finite measure and small exceptional sets.
exam_use: Useful for probability spaces when random variables are uniformly bounded.
pitfalls: Finite measure is essential; bounded pointwise convergence on an infinite measure space need not preserve integrals.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Dominated convergence theorem
summary: If f_n converges pointwise a.e. to f and |f_n| is bounded by an integrable g, then integrals of f_n converge to the integral of f.
proof_idea: Apply Fatou to g + f_n and g - f_n, or to |f_n - f| after a standard argument.
exam_use: The main theorem for passing limits through expectations under a uniform integrable bound.
pitfalls: Pointwise boundedness is not enough; the dominating function must be integrable and independent of n.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Fatou's lemma
summary: For nonnegative functions, the integral of the liminf is at most the liminf of the integrals.
proof_idea: Let g_n be the infimum over the tail; g_n increases to the liminf and monotone convergence applies.
exam_use: Use for lower semicontinuity of expectations and to prove integrability of a limit from bounded expectations.
pitfalls: Fatou gives an inequality, not usually equality.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Holder's inequality
summary: If p and q are conjugate exponents, the integral of |fg| is bounded by the Lp norm of f times the Lq norm of g.
proof_idea: Normalize the functions and apply Young's inequality; handle zero norms separately.
exam_use: Used for moment interpolation, uniform integrability checks, and bounding expectations of products.
pitfalls: Remember the endpoint cases need separate interpretation.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Jensen's inequality for integrals
summary: For a convex function phi and a probability measure, phi(integral f) is at most integral phi(f), assuming the quantities are defined.
proof_idea: Use a supporting line to the convex function at the mean.
exam_use: Prelim workhorse for Lp comparisons, moment generating bounds, and conditional Jensen later.
pitfalls: Convexity direction matters; strict equality often encodes almost sure constancy under strict convexity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Minkowski's inequality
summary: The Lp norm satisfies the triangle inequality for p at least 1.
proof_idea: Apply Holder to |f+g|^p split as |f+g|^{p-1}|f| plus the analogous term for g.
exam_use: Use to prove Lp spaces are normed and to control sums of random variables in Lp.
pitfalls: For p below 1 this is not a norm inequality.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Monotone convergence theorem
summary: If nonnegative measurable functions increase pointwise to f, then their integrals increase to the integral of f.
proof_idea: The lower approximation definition of the integral makes the result direct.
exam_use: Use for exchanging sums and expectations of nonnegative random variables.
pitfalls: The monotone direction and nonnegativity are not optional unless you shift or dominate carefully.

section: 1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits
title: Two-point distributions as Chebyshev extremizers
summary: To prove sharpness of moment/tail inequalities, place small mass at boundary points and the rest at a value chosen to match moments.
proof_idea: Translate integral tools into expectation language; use extremal two-point distributions for sharpness, Jensen/Cauchy-Schwarz for inequalities, and MCT/DCT for limits.
exam_use: Exercises 1.6.3, 1.6.4, and 1.6.5.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Change of variables formula
summary: If X has law mu and f is measurable, then E[f(X)] equals the integral of f with respect to mu, when the positive or integrable conditions hold.
proof_idea: Prove for indicators, extend to simple functions, nonnegative functions, and signed integrable functions.
exam_use: Lets you compute expectations using the distribution rather than the underlying sample space.
pitfalls: Do not assume a density exists; the law may be discrete, continuous, singular, or mixed.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Common expectation computations
summary: Chapter 1 reviews expectations for exponential, normal, Bernoulli, Poisson, and geometric distributions.
proof_idea: Use densities or mass functions plus the change-of-variables formula; use series identities for discrete distributions.
exam_use: These examples are quick diagnostic checks for later characteristic-function and limit problems.
pitfalls: Parameterization varies, especially for geometric distributions; state your convention.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Expected value as Lebesgue integral
summary: Expectation is the integral of a random variable with respect to the probability measure.
proof_idea: All integral theorems transfer directly to expectations.
exam_use: This unifies discrete sums, density integrals, and abstract random variables.
pitfalls: An expectation can be undefined if positive and negative parts are both infinite.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Limit theorems for expectation
summary: Fatou, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and bounded convergence all have expectation versions.
proof_idea: Translate each integral theorem to the probability space.
exam_use: A prelim proof often hinges on naming exactly the right convergence theorem and verifying its hypotheses.
pitfalls: Do not cite dominated convergence when you only have bounded expectations; that is a uniform integrability issue, not automatic domination.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Linearity and monotonicity of expectation
summary: Expectation is linear for integrable random variables and monotone for ordered random variables.
proof_idea: Apply integral linearity and monotonicity.
exam_use: Use constantly in variance, covariance, martingale, and conditioning calculations.
pitfalls: Linearity does not require independence; product factorization does.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Inclusion-exclusion and Bonferroni
summary: The probability of a finite union can be expanded by alternating sums of intersection probabilities, with Bonferroni inequalities for truncations.
proof_idea: Apply expectation to sums/products of indicator variables.
exam_use: Useful for event-counting, occupancy-style estimates, and bounding union probabilities more sharply than the union bound.
pitfalls: The exact formula is finite; infinite versions require additional limiting arguments.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Markov and Chebyshev inequalities
summary: Markov bounds the probability of a nonnegative variable exceeding a threshold by its expectation over the threshold; Chebyshev applies this to powers or centered moments.
proof_idea: Use the indicator inequality a 1_{X >= a} <= X for Markov, then specialize with nonnegative functions.
exam_use: Core tool for convergence in probability, LLN proofs, and variance bounds.
pitfalls: Check nonnegativity and choose the right moment; a weak moment gives a weak tail bound.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Tail integral and nonnegative expectations
summary: For nonnegative random variables, expectations can often be computed or bounded through tail probabilities.
proof_idea: Write X as an integral of indicators {X > t} and apply Fubini/Tonelli.
exam_use: Important for moment bounds, heavy-tail examples, and Borel-Cantelli estimates.
pitfalls: Mind strict versus non-strict inequalities only at atoms; the integral is unaffected in many continuous-parameter formulas.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration
title: Sliding interval Fubini identity
summary: Write F(x+c)-F(x) as mu((x,x+c]), swap integrals, and observe each fixed t is counted for exactly c units of x.
proof_idea: Turn integrals into product-measure regions; use sections and Tonelli to swap order; interpret Stieltjes integrals geometrically.
exam_use: Exercise 1.7.4.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration
title: Layer-cake formula via product sets
summary: Represent g(x) as the vertical length of {(x,y):0 <= y < g(x)} and apply Tonelli to integrate sections.
proof_idea: Turn integrals into product-measure regions; use sections and Tonelli to swap order; interpret Stieltjes integrals geometrically.
exam_use: Exercise 1.7.2; tail integrals for nonnegative functions.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration
title: Sine integral via Fubini
summary: Represent sin(x)/x as an integral of e^{-xy} sin(x), integrate in x first, and bound the remaining exponentially damped tails.
proof_idea: Turn integrals into product-measure regions; use sections and Tonelli to swap order; interpret Stieltjes integrals geometrically.
exam_use: Exercise 1.7.5.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 1 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration
title: Stieltjes integration by parts with jump correction
summary: Interpret Stieltjes integrals as product-measure masses of order regions; the diagonal overlap contributes the product of jump masses.
proof_idea: Turn integrals into product-measure regions; use sections and Tonelli to swap order; interpret Stieltjes integrals geometrically.
exam_use: Exercise 1.7.3.
pitfalls: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Fubini and Tonelli theorem
summary: For nonnegative functions, iterated integrals and the product integral agree. For signed functions, the same holds when the absolute integral is finite.
proof_idea: Prove by indicators, simple functions, monotone convergence, then signed decomposition under integrability.
exam_use: Use to exchange sums/integrals/expectations and compute expectations by conditioning-like iterated integration.
pitfalls: Tonelli handles nonnegative functions even with infinite value; Fubini for signed functions needs integrability.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Product measures as preview of independence
summary: When a joint law factors as a product measure, the coordinates behave independently.
proof_idea: Rectangular probabilities factor, and extension carries this to generated sigma-fields.
exam_use: This prepares for Chapter 2 independence and for computing expectations of products.
pitfalls: Independence is a statement about the joint law, not merely about marginal distributions.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Product measures
summary: Given measure spaces, the product measure is the unique measure on the product sigma-field that agrees with products of measures on measurable rectangles.
proof_idea: Define on rectangles, verify premeasure properties, and extend.
exam_use: The measure-theoretic basis for joint distributions and independence.
pitfalls: A set in the product sigma-field can be much more complicated than a rectangle.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 1 PDF
title: Measurable sections
summary: For a measurable set in a product space, its x-section is measurable in the second space, and section measures are measurable functions of x.
proof_idea: Prove first for rectangles, then close the class under monotone operations.
exam_use: This is the technical engine behind Fubini and Tonelli.
pitfalls: Do not assume section results for nonmeasurable product subsets.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Independence of events
summary: Events A and B are independent when P(A intersect B) = P(A)P(B); finite families require the product formula for every subfamily.
proof_idea: The definition is designed so multiplication of probabilities replaces intersection probabilities.
exam_use: Use it as the base language for indicator arguments, Borel-Cantelli, and pairwise-versus-mutual independence examples.
pitfalls: Pairwise independence does not imply mutual independence; always check all finite subcollections when the statement says independent.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Indicators encode event independence
summary: Events A and B are independent if and only if their indicators 1_A and 1_B are independent random variables.
proof_idea: The indicators take only values 0 and 1, so checking their joint probabilities reduces to A, B, and complements.
exam_use: Useful when converting event problems into expectation or covariance calculations.
pitfalls: Independence of indicators is stronger than merely E[1_A 1_B] being computable; it is equivalent here because the variables are binary.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Random variables and sigma-field independence
summary: Random variables are independent exactly when their generated sigma-fields are independent; measurable variables inside independent sigma-fields are independent.
proof_idea: Translate events about X and Y into sets in sigma(X) and sigma(Y), then apply the definition of sigma-field independence.
exam_use: Lets you move cleanly between event, random-variable, and information-field formulations.
pitfalls: Do not confuse uncorrelated with independent; zero covariance is far weaker.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Functions of independent blocks
summary: Measurable functions of disjoint independent blocks remain independent.
proof_idea: Each function is measurable with respect to the sigma-field generated by its block; independent sigma-fields pass independence to their measurable variables.
exam_use: Essential for random walks: past maxima and future increments can be separated.
pitfalls: The functions may be complicated, but they cannot reuse variables from another block.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Independence of blocks
summary: Disjoint blocks built from independent families are independent after taking generated sigma-fields.
proof_idea: Group variables into sigma-fields generated by each block and use the independence extension theorem.
exam_use: Use this to show X_1 is independent of functions of X_2,...,X_n, or that future increments are independent of past events.
pitfalls: The blocks must be formed from disjoint subfamilies of an independent family.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Pi-lambda independence extension
summary: If independent collections are pi-systems, then the sigma-fields they generate are independent.
proof_idea: Fix all but one coordinate and show the class of sets preserving the product formula is a lambda-system; apply the pi-lambda theorem repeatedly.
exam_use: High-value proof method for showing independence from checking rectangles, half-lines, or finite-dimensional cylinder sets.
pitfalls: The checked classes must be pi-systems and must generate the desired sigma-fields.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Expectation factorization under independence
summary: If X and Y are independent, then E[h(X,Y)] is the product-measure integral; in particular E[f(X)g(Y)] = E[f(X)]E[g(Y)] under nonnegative or integrable hypotheses.
proof_idea: Use the product joint law and Fubini/Tonelli.
exam_use: Core tool for variance of sums, covariance checks, moment computations, and LLN proofs.
pitfalls: The factorization needs independence and integrability/nonnegativity; linearity of expectation alone is not enough for products.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Independent variables have product joint law
summary: If X_1,...,X_n are independent with laws mu_i, then the joint law of the vector is the product measure mu_1 x ... x mu_n.
proof_idea: Check equality on measurable rectangles and extend by the pi-lambda theorem.
exam_use: Turns independence questions into product-measure integration questions.
pitfalls: Matching marginal laws alone never proves independence; the joint law must factor.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Convolution of distribution functions
summary: For independent X and Y with CDFs F and G, P(X+Y <= z) = integral F(z-y) dG(y).
proof_idea: Integrate the indicator 1{x+y <= z} against the product law and evaluate the inner integral.
exam_use: Use for distribution of sums when densities are absent or mixed.
pitfalls: The notation dG means integration with respect to the probability measure induced by G, not ordinary differentiation unless a density exists.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Convolution density formula
summary: If X has density f and Y is independent with law G, then X+Y has density h(x)= integral f(x-y)dG(y); if Y has density g, h=f*g.
proof_idea: Start from the CDF convolution and use Tonelli to identify the derivative/density.
exam_use: Standard way to compute sums of exponentials, normals, and mixed variables.
pitfalls: Keep the order x-y straight and check that the variables are independent.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Standard convolution examples
summary: Independent exponentials with the same rate give gamma/Erlang sums; independent normals add means and variances.
proof_idea: Use the convolution density formula and complete the square for normals.
exam_use: These are quick recognition patterns for prelim computations and later CLT comparisons.
pitfalls: Normal parameters vary by text; in Durrett normal(mu,a) uses variance parameter a.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Constructing independent random variables
summary: Finite independent variables with prescribed laws live on a product space with coordinate maps; infinite sequences use infinite product measures and cylinder sets.
proof_idea: Define probabilities on rectangles/cylinders by products of the marginal laws and extend.
exam_use: Important when a proof begins with 'let X_1,X_2,... be independent with laws ...'; this construction justifies existence.
pitfalls: Infinite product constructions are subtler than finite products; do not treat all subsets of R^N as measurable.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Chebyshev inequality for sums
summary: If a centered sum has finite variance, P(|S|>x) <= Var(S)/x^2.
proof_idea: Compute or bound the variance, then divide by the square of the target scale.
exam_use: Exercise 2.5.12 pattern: dyadic Chebyshev bounds become summable with a logarithmic correction.
pitfalls: Chebyshev gives polynomial bounds; a log factor may be needed for summability.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Convergence in probability
summary: X_n -> X in probability means P(|X_n-X|>epsilon)->0 for every epsilon>0.
proof_idea: Translate the target into tail probabilities and show each tends to zero.
exam_use: Used throughout weak laws, maximal estimates, and large-deviation preliminaries.
pitfalls: It is weaker than almost-sure convergence and does not by itself control all subsequences pathwise.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Weak law example patterns
summary: Chapter 2 uses weak laws to control averages, random counts, and approximations where variance estimates or truncation make the error vanish.
proof_idea: Reduce the target to a normalized sum and apply Chebyshev, triangular arrays, or truncation.
exam_use: Use this as a mental index for prelim questions asking for convergence in probability.
pitfalls: First identify the normalization; the wrong scale can make a true statement look false.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Tail-sum moment test
summary: For p>0, E|X|^p is finite exactly when the tail probabilities P(|X|^p>n) are summable up to constants.
proof_idea: Compare the integral of the tail distribution with the integer tail sum.
exam_use: Exercise 2.5.2 pattern: a.s. term control plus Borel-Cantelli forces finite moments.
pitfalls: The tail variable is |X|^p, not |X|; keep the normalization consistent.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Weak law of large numbers
summary: For iid integrable variables with mean mu, S_n/n -> mu in probability.
proof_idea: Use truncation plus Chebyshev, or invoke the finite-mean weak law.
exam_use: Supports probability estimates for the contribution of all non-extreme summands.
pitfalls: Weak convergence controls high-probability behavior, not pathwise eventual behavior.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Dependent covariance weak law
summary: A zero-mean sequence can satisfy a weak law even when dependent, provided lag covariances are bounded above by r(k) with r(k)->0.
proof_idea: Expand E(S_n^2), group cross terms by lag, and use a finite-lag/tail split before applying Chebyshev.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.2 pattern: independence is not the real requirement; small average covariance is.
pitfalls: The hypothesis is an upper bound without absolute values, so use it to control E(S_n^2) from above rather than proving absolute summability.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: IID finite variance weak law
summary: If X_i are iid with finite variance and mean mu, then S_n/n converges to mu in probability.
proof_idea: Apply the L2 weak law with Var(S_n/n)=Var(X_1)/n.
exam_use: The canonical first LLN proof; use it whenever the problem gives second moments.
pitfalls: This proof does not cover infinite-variance variables, even when the mean exists.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Monte Carlo integration via WLLN
summary: For iid uniform U_i and integrable f on [0,1], the sample average n^{-1} sum f(U_i) converges in probability to integral_0^1 f.
proof_idea: Apply the finite-mean weak law to Y_i=f(U_i); if f is square-integrable, Chebyshev gives variance-scale error bounds.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.3 pattern: translate numerical integration into an average of iid random variables.
pitfalls: The finite-variance Chebyshev bound at threshold a/sqrt(n) is order Var(f(U))/a^2, not a vanishing bound unless the threshold is fixed.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: L2 weak law of large numbers
summary: For uncorrelated variables with bounded average variance, (S_n - E S_n)/n converges to 0 in L2 and hence in probability.
proof_idea: Compute Var(S_n) as the sum of variances and apply Chebyshev.
exam_use: Fastest LLN proof when second moments are available.
pitfalls: Independence is sufficient but not necessary; uncorrelatedness plus variance control is enough.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Weighted Cesaro variance weak law
summary: If uncorrelated variables satisfy var(X_i)/i -> 0, then n^{-2} sum_{i<=n} var(X_i) -> 0, so the centered average converges to 0 in L2.
proof_idea: Write var(X_i)=i a_i with a_i -> 0 and split the weighted Cesaro average into a finite initial block plus a small tail.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.1 pattern: use when variances grow sublinearly rather than being uniformly bounded.
pitfalls: Do not require identical distributions; the deterministic centering is E S_n/n, not necessarily a fixed mean.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Triangular array weak law
summary: For row-wise independent triangular arrays, convergence of sums follows when row variances are small and large jumps are controlled.
proof_idea: Use truncation indicators plus Chebyshev on the truncated centered row sum.
exam_use: This is the flexible form behind many LLN proofs with changing distributions.
pitfalls: Rows are independent within each n; do not assume one infinite sequence unless the problem states it.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Diverging truncated-mean weak law
summary: If xP(|X|>x)->0 but EX is infinite, the averages can still satisfy S_n/n - mu_n -> 0 with mu_n=E[X 1{|X|<=n}] diverging.
proof_idea: The truncation theorem controls fluctuations around the moving center; monotone convergence or tail integration identifies mu_n -> infinity.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.5 pattern: separate weak-law concentration from existence of a finite mean.
pitfalls: A moving centering sequence is not the same as convergence of S_n/n to a finite constant.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: General tail integral transform
summary: If H(x)=integral_{(-infty,x]} h(y)dy with h>=0, then E H(X)=integral h(y)P(X>=y)dy.
proof_idea: Write H(X) as an integral of h(y)1{y<=X} and apply Tonelli.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.7 pattern: derive exponential moment and tail identities without differentiating a distribution function.
pitfalls: Nonnegativity of h is what makes Tonelli immediate; signed h needs integrability checks.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: IID finite mean weak law
summary: If X_i are iid with E|X_1| < infinity and mean mu, then S_n/n converges to mu in probability.
proof_idea: Truncate at scale n, show the truncated centered average has small variance, and show large terms occur with vanishing probability.
exam_use: This is the weak law most often needed when only integrability is assumed.
pitfalls: Finite mean is enough for WLLN but the proof requires controlling both tails and centering errors.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Integer-valued tail-sum moment formulas
summary: For nonnegative integer-valued X, E X=sum_{n>=1}P(X>=n) and E X^2=sum_{n>=1}(2n-1)P(X>=n).
proof_idea: Write k as sum_{n<=k}1 and k^2 as sum_{n<=k}(2n-1), then apply Tonelli to indicators.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.6 pattern: convert moments into tail sums for discrete heavy-tail checks.
pitfalls: Use >= n for integer variables; continuous formulas use integrals with P(X>t) or P(X>=t), which differ only on negligible sets in many settings.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Maximum term negligibility
summary: For iid integrable variables, max_{i<=n}|X_i|/n goes to 0 in probability.
proof_idea: Use the union bound n P(|X_1| > epsilon n) and the tail property implied by integrability.
exam_use: Often proves that no single observation can dominate the average.
pitfalls: The statement can fail for heavy tails without finite first moment.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Oscillating heavy tails can have finite effective centering
summary: A distribution may have infinite absolute mean but a convergent truncated mean, allowing S_n/n to converge in probability to a finite constant.
proof_idea: Verify x P(|X|>x)->0, apply the truncation weak law, then show the truncated means converge, often by an alternating series test.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.4 pattern: cancellation in signs can produce a finite weak-law center even when E|X| is infinite.
pitfalls: Do not call the limiting center EX unless the positive and negative parts are both finite.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Positive-variable self-normalized weak law
summary: For nonnegative iid variables, if mu(s)/(s P(X>s))->infinity and b_n solves n mu(b_n)=b_n, then S_n/b_n -> 1 in probability.
proof_idea: Apply the triangular-array truncation theorem: large jumps vanish by the nu(s) condition, truncated variance is controlled by a tail-integral split, and the truncated mean equals b_n.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.9 pattern: choose the normalization from the truncated first moment rather than from a finite mean.
pitfalls: The proof depends on b_n tending to infinity and on controlling xP(X>x) relative to mu(x); do not assume finite mean.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Truncation method for weak laws
summary: Replace X_i by X_i 1{|X_i| <= cutoff}, prove a law for the bounded part, and show discarded large jumps are negligible.
proof_idea: Bound the probability of any large jump by a union bound and control the truncated variance or expectation.
exam_use: Main route from finite variance LLN to finite mean LLN.
pitfalls: Choose the cutoff at the scale of the sum, often n or epsilon n; a fixed cutoff usually proves the wrong statement.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Unfair fair game truncation scale
summary: For rare payoffs of size 2^k with probabilities about 1/(2^k k^2), truncation at b_n=2^{m(n)} can force S_n/(n/log_2 n) to converge to a negative constant despite EX=0.
proof_idea: Pick b_n so large jumps are rare and truncated variance is small, then compute the missing positive tail as the dominant negative centering.
exam_use: Exercise 2.2.8 pattern: a formally fair infinite-mean game can have typical averages far below its expectation.
pitfalls: The expectation calculation is not enough; the typical scale is determined by truncation and the tail beyond b_n.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Arbitrary random variables can be normalized to zero a.s.
summary: For any random variables X_n, one can choose deterministic c_n -> infinity so that X_n/c_n -> 0 almost surely.
proof_idea: Pick c_n large enough that tail probabilities at finitely many rational error levels are summable, then diagonalize with Borel-Cantelli.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.13 pattern: build a normalization after seeing the sequence rather than using moment assumptions.
pitfalls: The constants are not canonical and may grow very fast.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Independent trials occur infinitely often
summary: For independent events with non-summable probabilities, the events occur infinitely often with probability one.
proof_idea: Apply the second Borel-Cantelli lemma directly.
exam_use: Useful for coin-flip patterns, record events, and rare-event recurrence questions.
pitfalls: Verify mutual independence or an acceptable independent-block structure, not just pairwise independence.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Summable tail bounds imply a.s. eventual control
summary: If P(|X_n| > a_n) is summable, then |X_n| <= a_n eventually almost surely.
proof_idea: Apply first Borel-Cantelli to the events {|X_n| > a_n}.
exam_use: A common one-line finish after Markov, Chebyshev, or exponential bounds.
pitfalls: Summability of the bound matters; merely tending to zero gives convergence in probability, not a.s. eventual control.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Bernoulli convergence via Borel-Cantelli
summary: For independent Bernoulli variables X_n with P(X_n=1)=p_n, X_n -> 0 in probability iff p_n -> 0, and X_n -> 0 a.s. iff sum p_n < infinity.
proof_idea: Translate convergence to occurrence of {X_n=1}; use first or second Borel-Cantelli.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.11 pattern: reduce binary convergence questions to summability of success probabilities.
pitfalls: The a.s. direction needs independence for the divergent case.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Borel-Cantelli lemmas
summary: Summable event probabilities imply only finitely many occurrences; for independent events, divergent probability sums imply infinitely many occurrences.
proof_idea: The first lemma uses a union bound on the tail union; the second uses independence to control the probability of no future occurrence.
exam_use: A general retrieval alias for exercises that use either Borel-Cantelli direction.
pitfalls: The divergent direction requires independence or a suitable substitute.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: First Borel-Cantelli lemma
summary: If sum P(A_n) < infinity, then P(A_n infinitely often)=0.
proof_idea: Bound P(union_{n>=m} A_n) by the tail sum and let m go to infinity.
exam_use: Use to prove almost sure eventual bounds from summable probability estimates.
pitfalls: No independence is needed for the first lemma.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Second Borel-Cantelli lemma
summary: For independent events, if sum P(A_n)=infinity, then P(A_n infinitely often)=1.
proof_idea: Use independence to bound the probability that none of A_m,...,A_n occur by a product and then by an exponential of the negative probability sum.
exam_use: Use to prove infinitely many successes, recurrence-style events, and almost sure lower bounds.
pitfalls: Independence or a suitable replacement is essential; divergence alone is not enough.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Continuous mapping for convergence in probability
summary: If X_n -> X in probability and f is continuous, then f(X_n) -> f(X) in probability.
proof_idea: Localize X to a compact set with high probability, use uniform continuity there, and control the complement.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.2 pattern: prove mapping results directly without invoking subsequences.
pitfalls: Continuity only gives local uniform continuity after compact localization; global uniform continuity is not required.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: On countable spaces, convergence in probability implies a.s. convergence
summary: If Omega is countable with all subsets measurable, convergence in probability implies pointwise convergence on all atoms of positive probability, hence almost sure convergence.
proof_idea: A positive-mass point that fails pointwise convergence would keep an error probability bounded away from zero along a subsequence.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.12 pattern: use atoms to upgrade convergence modes.
pitfalls: This relies on countability; it is false on general probability spaces.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Exponential maxima live at log n scale
summary: For iid exponential-tail variables with P(X>x)=e^{-x}, limsup X_n/log n=1 and max_{m<=n}X_m/log n -> 1 almost surely.
proof_idea: Use Borel-Cantelli at thresholds (1+-epsilon)log n and estimate P(M_n <= (1-epsilon)log n) by an exponential bound.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.15 pattern: identify extreme-value scales via summable/nonsummable tail probabilities.
pitfalls: Pointwise limsup and running maximum convergence require slightly different lower-bound arguments.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Single observations versus maxima under n-normalization
summary: For iid Y_n, Y_n/n -> 0 a.s. needs E|Y_1|<infinity, max_{m<=n}Y_m/n -> 0 a.s. needs E(Y_1^+)<infinity, and max convergence in probability needs xP(Y_1>x)->0.
proof_idea: Apply Borel-Cantelli to |Y_n|>epsilon n or Y_n>epsilon n; for maxima in probability use 1-(1-p_n)^n ->0 iff np_n->0.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.17 pattern: distinguish two-sided single-term control from one-sided running-maximum control.
pitfalls: Almost sure and in-probability maxima have different tail requirements.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Independent supremum finiteness criterion
summary: For independent X_n, sup_n X_n is finite a.s. iff sum P(X_n>A)<infinity for some finite A.
proof_idea: Use first Borel-Cantelli for sufficiency and second Borel-Cantelli over integer thresholds for necessity.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.14 pattern: characterize boundedness of an independent sequence by summability of one tail level.
pitfalls: Independence is used only for the divergent necessity direction.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Kochen-Stone second-moment lower bound
summary: If sum P(A_n)=infinity, then P(A_n i.o.) is bounded below by a limsup ratio of squared expected counts to second moments of event counts.
proof_idea: Apply the second-moment inequality P(S_n>0) >= (ES_n)^2/E(S_n^2) to tail sums of indicators, then let the tail start go to infinity.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.10 pattern: prove lower bounds for infinitely-often events under dependence when full independence is unavailable.
pitfalls: The denominator uses all pairwise intersections; replacing it by product probabilities is only valid under independence.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Limsup event interpretation
summary: The event {A_n infinitely often} is limsup A_n = intersection_m union_{n>=m} A_n.
proof_idea: Rewrite 'infinitely often' as 'for every m there exists n>=m' and translate quantifiers to countable set operations.
exam_use: Needed before applying Borel-Cantelli and for a.s. convergence arguments.
pitfalls: Do not confuse limsup A_n with union_n A_n; limsup requires infinitely many occurrences.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Monotone variance subsequence a.s. upgrade
summary: For increasing nonnegative X_n with EX_n asymptotic to a n^alpha and variance O(n^beta), beta<2alpha, prove X_n/n^alpha -> a a.s. via sparse subsequences and monotonicity.
proof_idea: Use Chebyshev on n_k=floor(k^r) with r(2alpha-beta)>1, Borel-Cantelli on the subsequence, then squeeze intermediate n by monotonicity.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.18 pattern: turn variance bounds into a.s. asymptotics when monotonicity bridges gaps.
pitfalls: Without monotonicity, subsequence convergence alone would not control intermediate indices.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Poisson sums strong law with variable means
summary: For independent Poisson X_n with means lambda_n and m_n=sum_{k<=n}lambda_k -> infinity, S_n/m_n -> 1 almost surely.
proof_idea: Apply the random-series convergence criterion to (X_n-lambda_n)/m_n, using sum lambda_n/m_n^2 < infinity, then Kronecker's lemma.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.19 pattern: use variance summability with the natural cumulative-mean scale.
pitfalls: A naive subsequence proof can fail when the means have large jumps; the series proof handles arbitrary lambda_n.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Subsequence principle for convergence in probability
summary: X_n -> X in probability iff every subsequence has a further subsequence converging to X almost surely.
proof_idea: Choose a further subsequence with summable error probabilities and apply Borel-Cantelli; conversely use the topological subsequence criterion on probabilities.
exam_use: Exercises 2.3.4-2.3.5 pattern: reduce convergence-in-probability limit theorems to almost sure subsequences.
pitfalls: The almost sure subsequence depends on the original subsequence; this does not say the whole sequence converges a.s.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Fatou and dominated convergence from convergence in probability
summary: Fatou and dominated convergence extend from a.s. convergence to convergence in probability by passing to a.s. convergent subsequences.
proof_idea: Select a subsequence attaining the relevant liminf or violating the target limit, then extract an a.s. convergent further subsequence.
exam_use: Exercises 2.3.4-2.3.5 pattern: combine subsequence extraction with Fatou, DCT, bounded convergence, or uniform integrability.
pitfalls: For unbounded dominated-convergence variants, prove uniform integrability; convergence in probability alone is not enough for expectations.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Metric and completeness for convergence in probability
summary: The metric E[|X-Y|/(1+|X-Y|)] metrizes convergence in probability, and random variables modulo a.s. equality are complete under it.
proof_idea: Use the subadditivity of t/(1+t); for completeness choose a subsequence with summable jump probabilities and apply Borel-Cantelli to get an a.s. limit.
exam_use: Exercises 2.3.6-2.3.7 pattern: turn Cauchy in probability into an a.s. Cauchy subsequence, then pull the whole sequence back by Cauchy control.
pitfalls: The metric is on equivalence classes modulo a.s. equality; otherwise d(X,Y)=0 is only equality a.s.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: St. Petersburg almost-sure limsup explosion
summary: In the St. Petersburg game, X_n/(n log_2 n) has infinite limsup almost surely, so the in-probability normalization for sums is not an a.s. law.
proof_idea: For each M, the events {X_n >= M n log_2 n} have divergent probability sum and are independent; apply the second Borel-Cantelli lemma.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.20 pattern: rare giant jumps can destroy almost sure convergence even when weak-law scaling works.
pitfalls: Convergence in probability of S_n/(n log n) does not control limsup behavior.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Subsequence plus monotonicity upgrade
summary: Prove almost sure convergence along a sparse subsequence using Borel-Cantelli, then control gaps by monotonicity or maximal bounds.
proof_idea: Choose a subsequence making probabilities summable; bridge between subsequence times with deterministic or probabilistic estimates.
exam_use: Appears in strong law proofs and renewal estimates.
pitfalls: The bridge is the hard part; convergence on a subsequence alone usually does not imply full convergence.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Subsequence control upgraded to full control
summary: A sparse almost-sure estimate can be upgraded to all times when the gaps are controlled by monotonicity or a maximal inequality.
proof_idea: Choose a subsequence with summable error probabilities, then compare every intermediate index to the next subsequence point.
exam_use: Exercise 2.5.1 pattern: prove a rate on n_m=floor(m^alpha), then bridge the gaps.
pitfalls: Subsequence convergence alone is insufficient; the gap-control step carries the proof.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Threshold maxima infinitely-often criterion
summary: For iid X_n and increasing thresholds lambda_n, max_{m<=n}X_m>lambda_n i.o. is equivalent to X_n>lambda_n i.o., hence controlled by sum tail probabilities.
proof_idea: If individual threshold exceedances are summable, only finitely many observations can drive the maxima over increasing thresholds; if divergent, the individual exceedances occur i.o.
exam_use: Exercise 2.3.16 pattern: reduce running-maximum events to one-time exceedance events when thresholds increase.
pitfalls: The monotonicity lambda_n up to infinity is what prevents one early large value from causing infinitely many later exceedances.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Fourth-moment strong law shortcut
summary: If iid centered variables have finite fourth moment, then S_n/n -> 0 almost surely by summable fourth-moment bounds.
proof_idea: Bound E[S_n^4] by a constant times n^2 and sum P(|S_n| > epsilon n) over n.
exam_use: A quick SLLN proof when high moments are available.
pitfalls: This proof is convenient but much stronger than needed; finite first moment is enough for the iid SLLN.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: IID finite mean strong law
summary: If X_i are iid and E|X_1| < infinity with mean mu, then S_n/n -> mu almost surely.
proof_idea: Truncate variables, prove convergence for bounded/centered parts with maximal inequalities, and show tails are negligible via Borel-Cantelli.
exam_use: Central theorem of the chapter and a frequent prelim target.
pitfalls: Almost sure convergence is stronger than convergence in probability; the WLLN proof alone is not enough.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Kelly-style investment growth optimization
summary: For fixed portfolio fraction p, long-run log wealth converges to c(p)=E log(ap+(1-p)V); c is concave in p and endpoint derivatives decide whether the optimum is interior.
proof_idea: Write log wealth as a sum of iid log returns, apply the SLLN, then differentiate c(p) under the expectation and use concavity.
exam_use: Exercise 2.4.3 pattern: optimize almost-sure exponential growth by maximizing expected log return.
pitfalls: Maximizing expected wealth and maximizing expected log wealth are different criteria.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Kolmogorov convergence criterion
summary: If independent mean-zero variables have summable variances, then their series converges almost surely.
proof_idea: Apply the maximal inequality to tails of the series and use Cauchy convergence almost surely.
exam_use: Use for convergence of centered random series and as a strong-law building block.
pitfalls: Summable variances are sufficient, not necessary; do not mistake it for a complete characterization.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Kolmogorov maximal inequality
summary: For independent mean-zero variables with finite variances, the probability that partial sums exceed a threshold is bounded by the total variance divided by the threshold squared.
proof_idea: Stop at the first crossing and use independence of the future increment to control E[S_n^2] on crossing events.
exam_use: Main tool for upgrading variance summability to almost sure control of partial sums.
pitfalls: The variables must be centered and independent; this is stronger than ordinary Chebyshev for the final sum.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Multiplicative recursion becomes additive after logs
summary: If a process satisfies |X_n|=product_{k<=n}R_k with iid positive factors and E|log R_1|<infinity, then n^{-1}log|X_n| -> E log R_1 a.s.
proof_idea: Take logarithms and apply the finite-mean SLLN; compute the constant from the distribution of the radial factor.
exam_use: Exercise 2.4.2 pattern: random scaling problems reduce to an SLLN for log radii.
pitfalls: Check integrability near zero before applying the SLLN to logarithms.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Renewal reward ratio via SLLN
summary: For cycles with iid working time X_i and downtime Y_i, the long-run working fraction is E X_i/(E X_i+E Y_i).
proof_idea: Apply the SLLN to cycle lengths X_i+Y_i and rewards X_i, invert cycle time to get N(t)/t, and squeeze the incomplete final cycle.
exam_use: Exercise 2.4.1 pattern: renewal reward limits are ratios of mean reward to mean cycle length.
pitfalls: Account for the possible incomplete cycle at time t; its contribution is negligible after division by t.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Strong law normalization habits
summary: Strong laws usually show a normalized centered sum tends to zero almost surely, then add back deterministic centering.
proof_idea: Write S_n/n - mu as n^{-1} sum (X_i - mu) and focus on partial-sum control.
exam_use: Helps organize proofs for arrays, subsequences, and renewal counting.
pitfalls: Centering must be finite; if the mean is infinite, S_n/n behaves differently.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Cauchy criterion in probability
summary: Convergence in probability implies the sequence is Cauchy in probability, uniformly over sufficiently far tails.
proof_idea: Apply the definition to pairs S_m,S_n after both indices are large.
exam_use: Starting point for proving Levy's theorem.
pitfalls: Cauchy in probability alone does not immediately give a.s. convergence without extra structure.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Independent increments in partial sums
summary: For sums of independent variables, disjoint blocks of increments are independent.
proof_idea: Events determined by X_{m+1},...,X_j are independent of sums using X_{j+1},...,X_n.
exam_use: Used inside first-crossing arguments and block decompositions.
pitfalls: The original variables must be independent; identical distribution is not needed.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Independent series variance test
summary: For independent centered summands, finite total variance is enough for almost-sure convergence.
proof_idea: Check that the series of variances is bounded by a known summable numerical series.
exam_use: Exercise 2.5.3 pattern: trigonometric factors are harmless when bounded by 1.
pitfalls: Do not require identical distributions; only independence, centering, and summable variances.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Kronecker lemma for normalized sums
summary: If sum x_n/n converges, then n^{-1} sum_{k<=n} x_k -> 0.
proof_idea: Apply summation by parts to convert convergence of weighted terms into Cesaro decay of partial sums.
exam_use: Turns convergence of a random series into a strong law for averages.
pitfalls: The weights must increase to infinity; here the natural weight is n.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Levy maximal inequality for independent increments
summary: A maximum of partial increments is controlled by the terminal increment times a lower bound on remaining-tail probabilities.
proof_idea: Stop at the first crossing; if the later tail is small, the full terminal sum must still be large.
exam_use: Core tool for Levy's theorem and convergence-in-probability to almost-sure convergence upgrades.
pitfalls: The independence is between the first-crossing event and the future tail increment.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Random power-series radius
summary: The radius of sum X_n z^n is 1/limsup |X_n|^{1/n}; for iid coefficients this is decided by E log^+ |X_1|.
proof_idea: Use the root test and Borel-Cantelli on events log^+|X_n| > epsilon n.
exam_use: Exercise 2.5.8 pattern: logarithmic tails determine random analytic radius.
pitfalls: Nonzero coefficients must occur infinitely often to rule out a larger radius.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Truncated centering in random series
summary: After removing large jumps, convergence depends on whether the truncated expectations form a convergent deterministic series.
proof_idea: Separate X_n 1{|X_n| <= c} into its mean plus centered fluctuation.
exam_use: Prevents hidden deterministic drift from spoiling almost sure convergence.
pitfalls: A centered variance argument cannot control a divergent sum of means.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Large jumps in random series
summary: A necessary component for series convergence is that large jumps occur only finitely often, typically sum P(|X_n| > c) < infinity.
proof_idea: If large jumps occur infinitely often, the terms cannot tend to zero; Borel-Cantelli gives the precise almost sure statement.
exam_use: First diagnostic check in random-series problems.
pitfalls: Termwise convergence to zero is necessary but not sufficient for series convergence.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Kolmogorov three-series theorem
summary: For independent variables, convergence of sum X_n is characterized by tail probabilities, truncated means, and truncated variances.
proof_idea: Split each X_n into large jumps, truncated means, and centered truncated fluctuations; apply Borel-Cantelli and the variance convergence criterion.
exam_use: The main checklist for almost sure convergence of independent random series.
pitfalls: The theorem uses a fixed truncation level; changing the cutoff changes the conditions but not the spirit.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Two-series variance criterion
summary: Independent mean-zero variables with summable variances have an almost surely convergent series.
proof_idea: Use Kolmogorov's maximal inequality to make the tails Cauchy almost surely.
exam_use: Quickly solves centered series such as sum X_n sin(n pi t)/n.
pitfalls: The variables must be independent and centered; otherwise deterministic drift must be treated separately.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Rates for random series tails
summary: Once a random series converges, tail estimates can quantify how fast partial sums approach the limit.
proof_idea: Combine maximal inequalities with variance or moment bounds on the remaining tail.
exam_use: Useful when a problem asks not just convergence but an almost sure order estimate.
pitfalls: A convergence theorem alone may not give the requested rate; track the size of tail variances or probabilities.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Infinite mean changes LLN behavior
summary: When the mean is infinite or undefined, normalized sums can diverge or be controlled by rare large terms rather than averaging.
proof_idea: Use truncation to see which tail contribution dominates the normalization.
exam_use: Important for spotting when a finite-mean LLN theorem cannot be applied.
pitfalls: Do not center by EX if EX is not finite; the expression may be meaningless.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 2 PDF
title: Age and residual lifetime limits
summary: The limiting tail for age, residual life, or their joint event is an integrated tail of the interarrival distribution divided by mu.
proof_idea: Write a renewal equation for the event and apply the key renewal theorem to the forcing function.
exam_use: Exercises 2.6.4 and 2.6.6 pattern: identify the renewal interval straddling t.
