title: Modified potential kernels in recurrent dimensions
summary: Subtract a reference heat kernel before integrating over time; in d=1 this gives -|x-y| and in d=2 gives -(1/pi)log|x-y|.
proof_idea: Turn occupation times into integrals of transition densities, compute potential kernels by changes of variables or subtraction constants, and normalize constants through the distributional Laplacian.
exam_use: Using potential kernels when the full-space occupation integral diverges.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 9 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 9.6 Exercises: Green functions, occupation densities, and potential-kernel normalization
title: Gamma change of variables for transient potential kernels
summary: For d>=3, integrate the heat kernel over time and use s=|x-y|^2/(2t) to get Gamma(d/2-1)/(2 pi^{d/2}) |x-y|^{2-d}.
proof_idea: Turn occupation times into integrals of transition densities, compute potential kernels by changes of variables or subtraction constants, and normalize constants through the distributional Laplacian.
exam_use: Green-function and expected occupation-density calculations.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Killed-domain Green function
summary: For Brownian motion killed on leaving D, G_D(x,y) is the density satisfying E_x integral_0^tau f(B_t)dt = integral_D G_D(x,y)f(y)dy.
proof_idea: Subtract the expected potential contributed after exit from the full-space potential.
exam_use: The bridge between Brownian occupation times and Poisson's equation.
pitfalls: The Green function depends on the domain and boundary killing, not only on |x-y|.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Brownian potential kernel in R^d
summary: The expected occupation density of Brownian motion is the potential kernel G(x,y), proportional to |x-y|^(2-d) for d>=3 and to -log|x-y| in d=2 after domain adjustment.
proof_idea: Integrate the Gaussian transition density over time and evaluate the resulting radial integral.
exam_use: Use to turn occupation-time expectations into spatial integrals.
pitfalls: In recurrent dimensions the full-space occupation integral can diverge, so constants and killed domains matter.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 9 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 9.7 Exercises: Poisson equation and exit-time moment recursions
title: Exit-time moment recursion
summary: For m_n(x)=E_x tau^n, the Markov property or Dynkin formula gives (1/2)Delta m_n=-n m_{n-1} with zero boundary values.
proof_idea: Identify exit-time moments by applying the Poisson representation recursively: the nth moment solves a Poisson equation with source n times the previous moment.
exam_use: Computing higher moments of Brownian exit times.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 9 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 9.7 Exercises: Poisson equation and exit-time moment recursions
title: Second exit-time moment Poisson system
summary: First solve (1/2)Delta v=-1 for v=E tau; then solve (1/2)Delta w=-2v for w=E tau^2, both with zero boundary values.
proof_idea: Identify exit-time moments by applying the Poisson representation recursively: the nth moment solves a Poisson equation with source n times the previous moment.
exam_use: Exercise 9.7.1 and second-moment exit-time computations.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Expected exit time solves Poisson's equation
summary: Taking g=1 gives v(x)=E_x tau, which solves 1/2 Delta v=-1 in G with zero boundary values at regular points.
proof_idea: This is the Poisson representation specialized to unit source.
exam_use: A common prelim computation: guess radial v in a ball and verify by Laplacian and boundary values.
pitfalls: Finite expectation needs domain control; unbounded domains may have infinite exit time.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Poisson solution vanishes at regular boundary points
summary: If G and g are bounded and y is a regular boundary point, then v(x)=E_x integral_0^tau g(B_t)dt tends to zero as x tends to y from inside G.
proof_idea: Regularity makes tau small with high probability; boundedness controls the remaining tail.
exam_use: Use to verify the zero boundary condition for occupation-time solutions.
pitfalls: Regularity and boundedness assumptions are doing real work here.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Poisson equation representation
summary: For 1/2 Delta u=-g in G with zero boundary data, any bounded solution must be v(x)=E_x integral_0^tau g(B_t)dt.
proof_idea: The process u(B_t)+integral_0^t g(B_s)ds is a local martingale up to tau; stop and let t approach tau.
exam_use: Use for expected occupation times, expected exit times, and source-term boundary problems.
pitfalls: Sign conventions vary; here the PDE is 1/2 Delta u=-g for a positive occupation formula.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Ball Green function by Kelvin transform
summary: For the unit ball in d>=3, G_D(x,y)=G(x,y)-|y|^(2-d)G(x,y/|y|^2); a logarithmic analog holds in d=2.
proof_idea: The Kelvin-reflected singularity makes the boundary value cancel on |x|=1.
exam_use: Use for explicit occupation densities in balls.
pitfalls: The formula has a singular-looking y=0 case that must be handled by a limit or separately.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Half-space Green function by reflection
summary: For the half-space, the killed Green function equals the full-space potential at y minus the full-space potential at the reflected point y_bar.
proof_idea: Use the reflection principle/image method so the difference vanishes on the boundary.
exam_use: Compute occupation times before exiting a half-space.
pitfalls: The reflected term has the same singularity mirrored across the boundary, not at the original y.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 9 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments
title: Finite-gauge boundary Feynman-Kac uniqueness
summary: Finite bounded gauge controls the terminal exponential weight and kills the tail term, giving u(x)=E_x[f(B_tau)exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds)].
proof_idea: Use exponential weighting for elliptic equations with potential, check the gauge before applying Feynman-Kac, and detect blow-up through interval eigenvalue thresholds.
exam_use: Uniqueness of bounded Schrodinger Dirichlet solutions.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 9 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments
title: Gauge integrability condition
summary: Before applying boundary Feynman-Kac with positive potential, check w(x)=E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds); bounded c alone does not ensure finiteness.
proof_idea: Use exponential weighting for elliptic equations with potential, check the gauge before applying Feynman-Kac, and detect blow-up through interval eigenvalue thresholds.
exam_use: Avoiding false Schrodinger Feynman-Kac representations.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 9 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments
title: Interval eigenvalue threshold for exponential exit times
summary: For tau exiting (-a,a), solve (1/2)u''+gamma u=0 with boundary value one; finiteness holds exactly below gamma=pi^2/(8a^2).
proof_idea: Use exponential weighting for elliptic equations with potential, check the gauge before applying Feynman-Kac, and detect blow-up through interval eigenvalue thresholds.
exam_use: Explicit exponential exit-time moments and gauge blow-up examples.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 9 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments
title: Schrodinger weighted martingale
summary: For (1/2)Delta u+c u=0, Ito plus the finite-variation product rule makes u(B_t)exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds) a local martingale before exit.
proof_idea: Use exponential weighting for elliptic equations with potential, check the gauge before applying Feynman-Kac, and detect blow-up through interval eigenvalue thresholds.
exam_use: Boundary Feynman-Kac formulas for elliptic equations with potential.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 9 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 9.8 Exercises: Schrodinger equation, gauge integrability, and exponential exit moments
title: Local exit expansion for smooth Schrodinger representations
summary: Stop at a small ball, expand v(B_sigma) and exp(integral_0^sigma c(B_s)ds), then divide by E sigma to get (1/2)Delta v+c v=0.
proof_idea: Use exponential weighting for elliptic equations with potential, check the gauge before applying Feynman-Kac, and detect blow-up through interval eigenvalue thresholds.
exam_use: Verifying smooth Schrodinger Feynman-Kac formulas solve the PDE.
pitfalls: Check boundedness, regularity, exit-time finiteness, boundary regularity, and gauge hypotheses before reusing Chapter 9 Brownian/PDE patterns.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Gauge function for exponential lifetime integrability
summary: The gauge w(x)=E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds) measures whether the Schrodinger Feynman-Kac representation is finite.
proof_idea: Local exponential exit-time bounds plus a covering argument propagate finiteness through connected domains.
exam_use: Check this before asserting uniqueness or boundary representations with exponential weights.
pitfalls: Finiteness at one point and boundedness over the domain require separate hypotheses in the chapter.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Gauge finiteness propagates on connected domains
summary: If G is connected and the gauge w is not identically infinite, then w(x)<infinity for every x in G; if |G|<infinity, w is bounded.
proof_idea: Local comparison across balls gives openness of the finite set, and connectedness plus small-set control gives closed propagation.
exam_use: Use to justify uniform integrability in Schrodinger uniqueness proofs.
pitfalls: Connectedness matters; separate components need separate checks.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Exponential exit moment from an interval
summary: For tau exiting (-a,a), E_x exp(gamma tau)=cos(x sqrt(2 gamma))/cos(a sqrt(2 gamma)) when 0<gamma<pi^2/(8a^2), and is infinite at or above the threshold.
proof_idea: Solve the ODE 1/2 u''+gamma u=0 with boundary value one and use the gauge/uniqueness result below the first eigenvalue.
exam_use: A high-yield explicit computation connecting Brownian exit times with eigenvalues.
pitfalls: The threshold is the first Dirichlet eigenvalue for the interval; at the threshold the denominator vanishes.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Schrodinger boundary condition at regular points
summary: At regular boundary points, the Schrodinger representation tends to the boundary value f(y).
proof_idea: Split paths by small exit time and nearby exit location, then control the remaining event with bounded gauge.
exam_use: Use to finish verification of boundary data for the weighted representation.
pitfalls: The exponential weight adds a tail-control requirement absent from the plain Dirichlet problem.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Schrodinger Dirichlet representation
summary: Under bounded connected domain, bounded continuous f and c, and finite gauge, the bounded solution is v(x)=E_x[f(B_tau) exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds)].
proof_idea: Stop the weighted martingale, use boundedness of the gauge for uniform integrability, and pass to the exit time.
exam_use: Use for boundary-value problems with killing or creation potentials.
pitfalls: Without the finite-gauge assumption, the formula may be infinite or uniqueness may fail.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Schrodinger boundary-value martingale
summary: For 1/2 Delta u+c(x)u=0 in G, u(B_t) exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds) is a local martingale up to the exit time.
proof_idea: Apply Ito's formula and the product rule; the PDE cancels the drift after weighting by the exponential potential.
exam_use: Foundation for boundary Feynman-Kac formulas and exponential exit-time moments.
pitfalls: The sign of c controls integrability; positive c can make the expectation infinite.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Positive potential can destroy bounded representation
summary: For G=(-a,a), c=gamma>0, and boundary value 1, the ODE solution exists only away from eigenvalue obstructions and exponential exit moments can blow up.
proof_idea: Solve 1/2 u''+gamma u=0 with boundary values and inspect zeros of cos(a sqrt(2 gamma)).
exam_use: Use as a warning that Feynman-Kac with positive potential needs integrability assumptions.
pitfalls: Bounded c does not automatically imply E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds) is finite.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Holder potential gives C2 Schrodinger solution
summary: With the chapter's bounded-domain/gauge assumptions and Holder continuous c, the Schrodinger representation is C2 and solves 1/2 Delta v+c v=0.
proof_idea: Reduce locally to Poisson-equation regularity with source c(x)v(x).
exam_use: Use to upgrade the probabilistic representation to a classical solution.
pitfalls: Smoothness is interior; boundary regularity is a separate issue.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Small-set exponential exit bound
summary: For each theta>0, sufficiently small open sets H have uniformly bounded E_x exp(theta tau_H).
proof_idea: Use occupation-time/tail bounds for Brownian exit from small-measure sets and sum the exponential series.
exam_use: Technical tool that makes the gauge theorem work.
pitfalls: Small Lebesgue measure is the key condition, not merely small diameter in the statement.
